events.content:
[{"role": "question", "text": "Andy Barish [4634]: guys yeah i just wanted to um maybe finish up on never ending possible just kind of moving it up how do you see the life cycle kind of of that promotion you know as it starts to lapse a year ago launch and um you know i guess um i guess with the new the new sauce do you expect um you know things to continue and and then also Just some color on the comparisons from a year ago. Just does October and November get a little bit tougher than September?", "speaker": "Andy Barish", "title": "Analyst at Jefferies"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Rick Cardenas [4668]: So, Andy, I'll talk about the length of Olive Garden's Never Any Possible. So, yes, we added four weeks to last year. It was three weeks more than our plan because we had always planned to add a few more weeks over time to Never Any Possible. Now, it is a 12-week promotion, so we're doing some things to kind of juice it up during the time. One of them is adding this new sauce, and we're adding the media to introduce the new sauce, which will coincide with last year's launch of Never Any Possible. That said, you know, it probably will wear off a little bit towards the end when the last year's Never Any Possible was still going stronger, but we believe that never-ending possible is better than not having never-ending possible during that time right before Thanksgiving. So we don't anticipate having every week that we run never-ending possible this year being positive to last year because probably towards the tail end of this year's never-ending possible, it's going to still be running on a little bit of strength of last year's NEPB. And I'll let Raj talk about kind of the forward kind of compares.", "speaker": "Rick Cardenas", "title": "President and CEO"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Raj Vinan [4740]: Yeah, I think when you think about where we were last year, we started to see some softness as we got into the fall of last year, especially as we got into mid to late October across all our brands. And, in fact, last year, never-ending possible preference went up significantly from the year before, and that probably helped that never-ending possible hold up well. But outside of Olive Garden, we started to see other brands did see some softness as we get into \u2013 October and then into November. So the compares actually get a little bit better, not significantly though, but there was some softness last year.", "speaker": "Raj Vinan", "title": "CFO"}]
---
[{"role": "question", "text": "Johnny Vanko [4792]: Hi, thank you. When you look at Olive Garden performance over the last couple of quarters, what really distinguishes in your mind Kind of the better markets from the slower markets, maybe even within those markets, what kind of distinguishes a better store than a store that's not as performing as well? I mean, is it income-driven? Is it demographics-driven? Or there may be certain areas of the country where you have a lot more incremental competition. Is there a way for us to kind of look below the averages here? and say, hey, here's kind of a class of underperforming stores. And more interestingly, do you have anything yourselves, any tools that you can do to pull some of the underperforming stores closer to average or above average?", "speaker": "Johnny Vanko", "title": "Analyst at JP Morgan"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Rick Cardenas [4838]: Yeah, John, I'll use Olive Garden as the example since it's a big part of our system. But let's just, in geography, there are parts of the country that actually were softer than other parts of the country. So Florida, for example, Texas a little soft in the quarter. You know, Florida, if you think about the travel component I mentioned, and if you listen to the theme parks and you listen to Disney and what happened in the summer, it was a slower summer. Texas, as I said, we had a lot of storms, got the hurricane hit in Houston. We have over 60 restaurants in Houston across all of Darden. That said, when you really look at performance across geography, the difference is the quality and commitment of the management team. and are they executing our standards the way we're supposed to execute our standards? I'll give you one. Olive Garden has been focusing on offering a guest a refill. We know that if you're offered a refill, whether or not you accept the refill, your value, your intent to return, everything else goes way up. If you're not, it doesn't. So that, we know, is one of the big drivers of Olive Garden performance. At Longhorn, if your steak is cooked correctly, and by the way, they do a great job in cooking the steaks correctly. If it's cooked correctly, your intent to return is huge. If it's not, it's a lot lower. So I will tell you that if the management team is trained and ready to go and the general manager or the managing partner is leading that team to our standards, they do really well. If they don't, then they don't. And so we've got our directors of operations in those restaurants to help those managers. But, you know, we offer the same menu across all of our restaurants. We hire the same people across all of our restaurants. It's that management team that makes the difference. And we believe we have the best management teams in the industry and the best general managers in the business. So we're really confident that we continue to execute. We win. Thank you.", "speaker": "Rick Cardenas", "title": "President and CEO"}]
---
[{"role": "question", "text": "Jim Sanderson [4971]: Hey, thanks for the question. I wanted to go back to the discussion on pricing. I think that you had taken down your Create Your Own Pasta promotion price point by about 4% over the summer. Just wondering if you could tell us a little bit more about how that worked out in the quarter and whether you expect to feature more discounted promotions going forward, similar to what we saw over the summer.", "speaker": "Jim Sanderson", "title": "Analyst at North Coast Research"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Rick Cardenas [4992]: Hey, Jim, you know, if you think about Create Your Own Pasta, We had priced it up kind of late in last fiscal year to, I think, a little bit over $13. And we thought, you know what, maybe we should have kept it at $12.99. So it's actually at $12.99 on our menu today. So it wasn't, I don't think it was a 4% reduction. Maybe it was, but, you know, it went down maybe 40, 50 cents, so probably so. But we still took our pricing across. And so we decided that Never Any Pasta Bowl, which is a great value offer every day, probably it makes more sense at $12.99 than at $13.49. So we were able to move that price other places. So when you think about what we're doing in the future with it, we believe it's going to stay around that price point. But we'll continue to refresh that offer and continue to provide reasons for guests to come and create your own pasta offer. That's $12.99, which is not limited time. It's every day. But we'll also continue to talk about some of the great value we have on the rest of the menu and maybe some limited time offers to get people in so they can see the great value on the rest of the menu. If there's any other things on pricing, Rush, did I miss anything on the price?", "speaker": "Rick Cardenas", "title": "President and CEO"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Raj Vinan [5069]: I think you got it.", "speaker": "Raj Vinan", "title": "CFO"}]
---
[{"role": "question", "text": "Jim Sanderson [5072]: Just a quick follow-up. When you did normalize that price point back to $12.99, did you notice any consumer reaction? Good or bad?", "speaker": "Jim Sanderson", "title": "Analyst at North Coast Research"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Rick Cardenas [5083]: Yes, we did. You know, it performed better than it was doing at 13 plus. Preference went up a little bit. And our performance in June was, I think, a little bit better than it was in May. So at Olive Garden.", "speaker": "Rick Cardenas", "title": "President and CEO"}]
---
[{"role": "question", "text": "Jim Sanderson [5099]: Yep. Understood. Last quick question. Can you give us an estimate of the dollar budget for G&A for the year given you had some exceptional expenses last year for comparison purposes?", "speaker": "Jim Sanderson", "title": "Analyst at North Coast Research"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Raj Vinan [5110]: Yeah, I think we said this in the initial guidance. It hasn't changed since then. We're probably looking at somewhere close to $450 million. With the first quarter being $125 million, you can expect the rest to be more in that $105 million to $110 million any given quarter.", "speaker": "Raj Vinan", "title": "CFO"}]
---
[{"role": "question", "text": "Brian Vaccaro [5134]: Hi, thanks so much. Just two quick ones for me. On your existing off-premise business at Olive Garden, can you remind us what percent of that is small order takeout versus larger group or catering type orders?", "speaker": "Brian Vaccaro", "title": "Analyst at Raymond James"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Rick Cardenas [5149]: Yeah, it's about 80% small order and about 20% kind of catering side. Okay, great.", "speaker": "Rick Cardenas", "title": "President and CEO"}]
---
[{"role": "question", "text": "Brian Vaccaro [5157]: And just to clarify on your comments on never ending pasta, Rick, did you say that the overall sales mix on NEP was up or was that trade up, you know, adding on proteins that was up? I'm just curious, what percentage of your customers end up trading up and adding proteins versus staying at that starting price point?", "speaker": "Brian Vaccaro", "title": "Analyst at Raymond James"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Rick Cardenas [5177]: Yeah, I would say two things. One, the overall preference is up slightly for never ending pasta bowl. In some markets of the country, it's up a lot. but it's up slightly, but the trade-up is up more than our overall preference is up. And the trade-up is about 65, 60 to 65% of the people order the protein on top of the pasta, which is up versus last year.", "speaker": "Rick Cardenas", "title": "President and CEO"}]
---
[{"role": "question", "text": "Brian Vaccaro [5205]: Perfect. Thank you.", "speaker": "Brian Vaccaro", "title": "Analyst at Raymond James"}, {"role": "answer", "text": "Courtney Aquila [5218]: That concludes our call. I want to remind you that we plan to release the second quarter results on Thursday, December 19th, before the market opens for the conference call to follow. Thank you for participating in today's call.", "speaker": "Courtney Aquila", "title": "Director of Investor Relations"}]
